title: Keating calls for radical retirement rethink
full_text:
Former Prime Minister Paul Keating has proposed a sweeping overhaul of Australia’s retirement income system, arguing superannuation funds should play a much larger role in helping retirees access the Age Pension and manage their retirement.
According to reporting from The Australian Financial Review, Keating – a pioneer in modern superannuation – raised the proposal following discussions with Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, describing it as a “big opportunity” to simplify retirement by bringing together two systems that currently operate largely independently.
He argued retirees should no longer have to navigate separate processes for their superannuation and Centrelink entitlements.
The AFR reported Keating wants superannuation funds to become the primary point of contact for retirees, with greater integration between funds and Centrelink allowing Australians to view their retirement savings and Age Pension entitlements through a single system.
While the proposal would not privatise the Age Pension, it would require much closer cooperation between government and the superannuation sector through increased data sharing and administration.
The proposal has already found support among several prominent industry figures, with The Australian reporting that Cbus chair and National President of the Labor Party, Wayne Swan, backed closer cooperation between superannuation funds and Centrelink, arguing stronger coordination would make retirement easier for Australians receiving both superannuation income and a part Age Pension.
Aware Super chief executive Deanne Stewart and Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia (ASFA) chief executive Mary Delahunty also supported greater integration, while AustralianSuper chief executive Paul Schroder was reported as having previously advocated for stronger data sharing between government and super funds to reduce complexity for retirees.
Coalition figures have questioned whether privately managed superannuation funds should assume responsibilities traditionally carried out by government, with The Australian reporting concerns centred on governance, privacy and maintaining a clear distinction between taxpayer-funded services and privately managed retirement savings.
Keating’s latest intervention reflects the philosophy that has underpinned his approach to super since compulsory contributions were introduced in the early 1990s.
As the architect of Australia’s compulsory super system, he has long argued the Age Pension, compulsory super and voluntary savings should operate as a single retirement income framework rather than as disconnected parts of the system.
After the Superannuation Guarantee reached 12 per cent last year, Keating described the increase as completing the retirement savings system first envisaged during his time in government, arguing younger Australians would now spend their entire working lives receiving the full compulsory employer contribution.
Although he has consistently defended compulsory super, Keating has also been one of Labor’s most prominent critics on retirement policy, opposing the government’s original Division 296 tax proposal on the basis that taxing unrealised capital gains and failing to index the $3 million threshold risked undermining confidence in the system.
Rather than revisiting the long-running debate over contribution rates or tax concessions, Keating’s latest proposal turns attention to how Australia’s retirement income system could operate once workers leave the workforce, placing superannuation funds at the centre of a more integrated approach to retirement.
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation and Investments
page_title: Keating calls for radical retirement rethink - Super Review
